title: Elymus caninus
intro: Elymus caninus, also known as dog's tail grass or dog's tail, is a perennial grass species native to Europe and Western Asia, characterized by its distinctive, hairy inflorescence resembling a dog's tail. Traditionally, the plant has been used in folk medicine to treat a range of health issues, including digestive problems and skin conditions, thanks to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Elymus caninus include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and lignans, which have been shown to contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Elymus caninus is often prepared as a tea or tincture, and it can also be used in combination with other herbs to create infusions and decoctions that promote digestive health and soothe irritated skin.
taxonomy: Elymus caninus is classified as a species of grass in the family Poaceae. This species belongs to the genus Elymus, commonly known as the wheat-grasses or wild-wheat grasses. The Poaceae family is a large and diverse group of plants that also includes other grasses and cereals. Elymus caninus is native to Europe and parts of Asia, and is often found in dry grasslands and open woods. It is a perennial grass with a distinctive growth habit and a range of adaptations that allow it to thrive in a variety of environments.
